text: We present the case of a fourteen-year-old female with a one-year history of diffuse abdominal pain causing vomiting that worsened two weeks prior to being seen in our emergency department. Her medical history was significant for depression. On physical examination, a mass was palpable from the pigastrium to the umbilical area. CT scan revealed a mass that was contained in the stomach. This mass was characteristic of a trichobesaur.

During laparoscopy, the surgeon identifies a rigid stomach due to the mass within the gastric lumen. The lesser sac is opened to mobilize the stomach for laparoscopic surgery. The surgeon measures the stomach size to tailor a containment bag, preventing contamination of the peritoneal cavity.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to perform a gastrostomy, opening the stomach and revealing a thin gastric wall and components of the trichobezoar. The gastrostomy measures approximately ten centimeters in length. The surgeon carefully peels off the stomach wall and attempts to pull the bezoar into a specimen bag, taking care to avoid injuring the stomach wall.

The surgeon applies gentle traction to reduce the bezoar tail associated with Rapunzel syndrome, pulling it back into the gastric cavity from the small bowel. The tail is carefully maneuvered to ensure complete reduction, preventing its extension towards the ileocecal valve.

The surgeon places the specimen inside a retrieval bag, closes it, and removes it through a funnestal incision. The abdomen is visible, and the funnestal incision is closed and washed. The gastrotomy is closed using a monofilament absorbable suture in a running fashion, completing the closure in a single layer.
The stomach will be next tested for Ehrlich

The operative time was eighty minutes. The patient had an uneventful post-operative course, was able to be discharged in post-op day four. Trichobesolar is an atypical cause of gastric outlet obstruction. It needs to be suspected in patients with psychiatric disorders. A large trichobesolar can be managed laparoscopically with minimal contamination. A financial incision is always a good option to remove a large specimen with good cosmetic results.
